Asymptotic analysis analyzes an algorithm's time and space complexity, depending on the input size. It helps identify the performance of an algorithm concerning the input size and is used to compare the efficiency of different algorithms

Asymptotic Analysis does not measure the actual runtime of the algorithm. Rather, it evaluates the performance of an algorithm in terms of input size. It calculates how the time (or space) an algorithm takes increases with the input size

A greedy algorithm focuses on a solution using a step-by-step approach. It does not worry about the end goal or focus on the problem. Rather, it attempts to find the next best possible step that should be taken to move ahead

Choosing the best available option makes locally optimal solutions at each step and moves to get an optimum global value. It's not considered if the current best result will lead to the overall best result. The algorithm will never undo a previous decision, even if the choice...
Greedy algorithms are mostly used to get an approximate solution to a complex problem
then use other techniques to iteratively improve your solution. Greedy techniques are often used to find solutions to NP-hard problems. These are problems that are close to impossible to solve

Various Greedy methods are used for different optimization problems. It is also important to note that Greedy algorithms often fail to find the best or most optimal solution. However, greedy algorithms are extremely useful for finding approximate solutions to complex problems

Use the Pop method of the myStack object to remove and return elements from the top of the stack

Notice that first, there are elements added to the stack. The last element is 40. Immediately after the PUSH operation, there is a POP operation, which removes 40 from the stack. When you execute the code, the stack returns elements starting from 30. POP does not take a parameter
The exhibit below displays an example of the POP operation

The POP operation specifies removing the topmost item from the stack. The stack underflows condition if you try to perform the pop operation on an empty stack. You can use the is Empty() function to check if the stack is empty
This example pops elements from the stack. When you display the stack output, 40 will be displayed on the top. However, 40 is removed from the stack when the POP operation is performed

pairs.question: In the post-order traversal, the parent node is visited only after both children have been visited
First, we start processing from node A
Next, we process the right child node of D, H. Since H is a leaf node, we will move to the right child node of C, E
Since E does not have a left child node, we move to G, the right child and leaf node
Since this is a leaf node, we cannot move further. We then move to the right child node A, which is C
The program below uses the same inputs from the bulleted points above to display the outcome, which is A B C D F H E G
We finally complete the traversing of the binary tree. We started with A and completed at G
We process the left child node of D, F. Since it is a leaf node, we move to the right node
We then move one level below to the left node, B, which is the child of node A
We then process the left child node of C, D

Bubble sort compares each pair of array elements unless the whole array is sorted in ascending order

Bubble sort is a comparison-based algorithm that compares every pair of elements in the list and swaps them to get the smaller element in earlier position of the list. It follows a simple logic. It compares adjacent elements in a loop and swaps them if they are not in order
Bubble sort is named this way because, in this sorting method, the smaller elements gradually "bubble up" to the top of the list. Bubble sort should not be used with large or complex data sets
However, if the entire list starts in descending order, the time complexity increases because all elements must get into the ascending order, which will require many swapping operations

Along with the time complexity, an algorithm should be efficient enough to minimize the use of available space. Think of a scenario in which you have a system with limited memory. If the input data is large, more memory will be required for efficient operation execution

However, with the limitation of memory or space, the execution time will increase because the data cannot simultaneously fit into the small-sized memory. Even though you can measure time and space complexities separately, they are interdependent, as mentioned in the example above
It is the amount of memory required to perform operations in a program. Less space is required, and the more efficient the algorithm is

The algorithm requires space, also known as memory, to solve the problem. It is the amount of memory an algorithm needs to store as a function of its input size. It is also called auxiliary space. It can also be used to measure the performance of an algorithm

The space complexity of Merge Sort is O(N). This means that the algorithm requires additional memory to store the temporary sub -arrays that are created during the sorting process. In the worst-case scenario, this extra memory usage can be equal to the size of the input array

pairs.question: Infix notation is the commonly used notation to represent arithmetic expressions, in which the operators are written between the operands. However, this notation has a few limitations, mainly because it is ambiguous when multiple operators with different precedence are involved

One of the important applications of Stack is its use in conversion of infix to postfix. Because of the nature of LIFO (last in first out) and having just one opening to extract data, it is best suited to perform this conversion. Following are the steps to perform infix to pos...

One way to remove the am biguity and specify the order of evaluation explicitly is to convert the infix expression to postfix notation. Postfix notation, also called Reverse Polish notation, is a way of representing expressions in which the operators come after the operands. F...
The entire reason for converting infix into other forms is so that it is easier to calculate

) + ABC*D+ When it is the ')' operator, pop '+' and add it to the expression string / +/ ABC*D+ '/' has higher precedence than +, so push '/' into the stack E +/ ABC*D+E/+ Add E into the expression string and pop all the operators one by one and add them to the expression stri...

1. Reverse the input string. This step is necessary because in prefix notation, operators appear before their operands. Reversing the input string allows you to effectively read the expression from right to left
2. Examine the next element in the input
3. Add it to the output string if it is an operand. If it is a closing parenthesis, push it on the stack
4. If it is an operator, and if the stack is empty, push the operator on the stack. If the top of the stack has a closing parenthesis, push the operator on the stack
5. If it has the same or a higher priority than the top of the stack, push the operator onto the stack; first, pop the operator from the stack. Then, add the operator to the output string
6. If it is an opening parenthesis, pop it out of the stack and add it to the output string until a closing parenthesis is encountered. Pop and discard the closing parenthesis
7. If no more input exists, unstack the remaining operators and add them to the output string
8. Reverse and return the output string
First, reverse the given infix expression
Read the next token, which is "("
Read the next token, which is ")". You pop operators from the stack and add them to the output string until you reach the left parenthesis "("
Read the next token, which is "-". Since "-" has lower precedence than "/", you push it onto the stack
Read the next token, which is "/". Since "/" has the same precedence as "", you pop "" from the stack and add it to the output string. Then you push "/" onto the stack
Read the next token, which is "2"
Read the next token, which is "5"
Read the next token, which is the end of the expression

Node 1 has no child elements
Node 7 has one child node, 3
Node 8 has two child nodes, 7 and 9
Node 8 is the ancestor of 3, 5, and 14

One with one or two nodes underneath is the parent node. The node on the left is the left child. The node on the right is the right child. The binary tree starts with the root node, which has no parent node. Nodes with one or two child nodes are known as parent nodes

Several nodes have the same parent. For example, nodes 1 and 8 have node 2 as the parent. Nodes 7 and 9 have node 7 as the parent. Nodes 5 and 14 have node 9 as the parent node. Nodes that have the same parent are known as sibling parents
The nodes with no child nodes are known as leaf nodes
